professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been discussing how brands reach younger audiences this semester. Many companies now pay micro-influencers to feature products in ordinary-looking posts, often without a clear 'sponsored' label. Some marketers argue this feels authentic and drives real engagement, while others say hiding the paid relationship deceives followers. Should brands be required to clearly disclose every paid influencer post? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe disclosure should be mandatory. When followers can't distinguish an ad from a genuine recommendation, their trust is exploited for profit, especially among teenagers who take endorsements at face value. Transparency costs the brand almost nothing and preserves the platform's credibility.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Mandatory labels on every post go too far. Audiences today are savvier than we assume; most already recognize that a polished product feature is likely sponsored. Heavy-handed disclosure rules would bury smaller creators in paperwork while barely changing how anyone actually interprets the content.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: When a historic cathedral or temple is lost to fire or war, cities face a hard choice. Some preservationists insist on rebuilding an exact replica so the community keeps its landmark, while others argue a modern reconstruction is a fake that erases the honest scars of history. Should destroyed monuments be rebuilt as faithful replicas? Why or why not?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I think faithful reconstruction is worth it. A landmark anchors a community's identity, and its sudden absence leaves a wound that history books can't fill. Rebuilding with original techniques also revives craft knowledge that would otherwise vanish, so the replica becomes a living tradition rather than a hollow copy.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: A replica can never carry the authenticity of the original. Once the historic fabric is gone, what we rebuild is essentially a full-scale model, however skilled the craft. I'd rather stabilize the ruins as an honest record, since pretending nothing was lost distorts the very history we claim to honor.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been examining how democracies boost civic participation this semester. A handful of countries, like Australia, fine citizens who fail to vote, pushing turnout above ninety percent. Supporters say compulsory voting produces governments that reflect everyone, not just the motivated few, while critics counter that forcing reluctant, uninformed people to the polls cheapens the ballot. Should voting be made mandatory? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: Mandatory voting strengthens democracy more than it strains it. When turnout is nearly universal, politicians can no longer win by mobilizing a narrow, extreme base and ignoring everyone else. The small fine is a minor nudge, and citizens remain free to submit a blank ballot if they truly object.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: Forcing participation misunderstands what a healthy democracy needs. A vote cast only to dodge a penalty carries no genuine judgment, so we'd simply be padding turnout with uninformed guesses. Effort is better spent on civic education and easier registration, which raise participation because people actually want to engage.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: Remote communities increasingly face a dilemma about tourism. Opening a village to visitors can fund schools and clinics and give young people a reason to stay, yet a steady stream of outsiders often turns living traditions into staged performances and erodes the very culture tourists came to see. Should isolated communities welcome cultural tourism, or protect themselves from it? Why?

students.name: Paul
students.text: Cultural tourism, managed carefully, does more good than harm. The income lets a community preserve crafts and ceremonies that would otherwise fade as young people leave for cities. What matters is control: if the community sets the terms and limits visitor numbers, tourism becomes a tool for survival rather than exploitation.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: The costs usually outweigh the benefits once tourism takes hold. Sacred rituals get shortened into photo opportunities, and prices rise until locals can't afford their own town. Communities would be better served by targeted development aid that doesn't require selling their identity to strangers with cameras.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: More and more online retailers now use algorithms to set a different price for each shopper, based on browsing history, device, or location. Some analysts defend this as efficient, letting firms offer discounts to price-sensitive buyers, while others call it a hidden penalty on loyal or wealthier customers who never see the lower number. Is personalized pricing ethical? Why or why not?

students.name: Kelly
students.text: I believe personalized pricing crosses an ethical line. Charging people different amounts for an identical product, based on data they never agreed to share, treats shoppers as targets to squeeze rather than customers to serve. Without transparency, they can't even know they're being singled out.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Tailored pricing is just a modern version of discounts and coupons. Businesses have always charged what different segments will pay, and algorithms simply do it faster. As long as the practice is disclosed and no protected group is targeted, letting the market flex prices can actually widen access for buyers on tight budgets.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: We've been looking at how software shapes the products we own. Many devices now stop working when the manufacturer's servers shut down or refuse third-party repairs, so a phone or tractor becomes a brick the moment support ends. Advocates of 'right to repair' laws say owners should control their own hardware, while makers warn that opening the software invites security risks. Where do you stand?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think right-to-repair protections are overdue. When you buy a device, you should own it fully, not rent it on terms the maker can revoke by killing a server. Independent repair keeps products out of landfills and breaks the monopoly that lets companies charge whatever they like for a simple fix.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: In my opinion, the security concerns are too easily dismissed. Handing out the tools and code to bypass a device's protections also hands them to attackers, and average users can't judge which repair is safe. I'd support limited access for certified shops rather than forcing companies to open everything to anyone.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: This week, we've been exploring how the way we praise children shapes their motivation. Research suggests that telling a child 'you're so smart' can make them avoid challenges to protect that label, while praising effort — 'you worked hard on this' — encourages persistence. Yet some argue that constant effort-praise rings hollow when results are poor. Which kind of feedback actually helps students learn? Why?

students.name: Paul
students.text: I believe praising effort is far more productive. When children learn that persistence, not some fixed talent, drives improvement, they stop fearing hard problems and start treating mistakes as part of learning. A student convinced they're simply 'not a math person' gives up the moment things get difficult, and that belief becomes self-fulfilling.

students.name: Claire
students.text: In my opinion, we shouldn't swing entirely to effort-praise either. Children can tell when praise is disconnected from actual results, and rewarding effort that produced nothing teaches them that trying is enough. The most honest feedback names the effort but also points to what specifically improved, so encouragement stays tied to real progress.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: We've been discussing how cities balance mobility and livability this semester. A growing number of European cities have closed their historic centers to private cars, allowing only pedestrians, bikes, and transit. Supporters point to cleaner air and livelier streets, while critics warn that shop owners lose drive-in customers and that people with limited mobility are left stranded. Should cities ban cars from downtown cores? Why or why not?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I think car-free centers are a clear improvement. Where cities have tried it, foot traffic to shops often rises rather than falls, because pleasant streets invite people to linger and spend. Cleaner air and quieter public spaces benefit everyone, and delivery access or accessible transit can be built in for those who genuinely need to drive.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: In my opinion, blanket bans move too fast for most cities. Unless the transit network is already dense and reliable, cutting off cars strands elderly residents and workers who commute from the outskirts. I'd phase it in — expand bus lines and parking on the edges first, then restrict the core once real alternatives exist.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: We've been examining how people make choices under uncertainty this semester. Before buying almost anything now, many of us scan dozens of star ratings and stranger reviews first. Some psychologists argue this collective feedback helps us avoid bad decisions, while others warn it breeds herd behavior and lets a few fake or extreme reviews sway the crowd. Do online ratings improve our decisions? Why or why not?

students.name: Kelly
students.text: I believe ratings genuinely improve most decisions. No single person can test dozens of products, but the aggregated experience of thousands surfaces patterns a glossy advertisement would hide. As long as you read past the extremes and look for detailed, specific reviews, the wisdom of the crowd usually points you toward the better choice.

students.name: Andrew
students.text: In my opinion, ratings distort our judgment as often as they sharpen it. A flood of reviews is easily gamed with paid five-star posts, and a high average makes us stop thinking critically about whether the product suits our own needs. We end up outsourcing a personal decision to strangers whose priorities may differ from ours.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: Online anonymity is under fresh scrutiny. After waves of harassment and coordinated misinformation, several governments have floated rules requiring social platforms to verify every user's real identity. Some argue that attaching real names would curb abuse and make people accountable for what they post, while others warn it would endanger whistleblowers, activists, and ordinary people who need to speak freely. Should platforms require verified real identities? Why?

students.name: Paul
students.text: Requiring real identities would do more harm than good. Anonymity is exactly what lets whistleblowers expose wrongdoing and lets people in repressive places speak at all. Determined trolls will always find ways around verification, so the main effect would be to silence the vulnerable users who most need cover, not the abusers.

students.name: Claire
students.text: Verified identities are worth the trade-off for healthier platforms. Much of the cruelty and organized disinformation online thrives precisely because no one can be traced or held responsible. A system that confirms identity privately, without publishing real names, could deter the worst abuse while still shielding genuine activists behind pseudonyms.
